Mutaguchi|ja|牟田口元学}} | after = Abolished }} {{S-other}} {{Succession box | title = Head of [[Izumo-taishakyo]] | years = [[1882]]–[[1888]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Sonai (千家尊愛) }} {{Succession box | title = Head Priest of [[Izumo Taisha]] | years = [[1872]]–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{Succession box | title = 80th {{ill|Izumo no kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} | years = 1872–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{S-reg|jp}} {{Succession box | title = Baron | years = [[1884]]–[[1918]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Takamuni (千家尊統) }} {{End}} {{Izumo-taishakyo}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:Founders of religions]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese Shintoists]] 1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 出雲大社教] * {{Commons category-inline}} {{Clear}} {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} Chairman of the {{ill|Legal commission|ja|法律取調委員会}} | years = 1908 | before = Mutaguchi|ja|牟田口元学}} | after = Abolished }} {{S-other}} {{Succession box | title = Head of [[Izumo-taishakyo]] | years = [[1882]]–[[1888]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Sonai (千家尊愛) kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} | years = 1872–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{S-reg|jp}} {{Succession box | title = Baron | years = [[1884]]–[[1918]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Takamuni (千家尊統) }} {{End}} {{Izumo-taishakyo}} [[category:kk]][[category:Founders of religions]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese Shintoists]] {{Short description|Japanese politician and religious leader}} * {{Infobox officeholder | 人名 = 千家 尊福 | 各国語表記 = せんげ たかとみ | 画像 = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | 画像サイズ = 240px | 画像説明 = [[大礼服]]で[[正装]]した千家 | 国略称 = {{JPN}} | 生年月日 = [[1845年]][[9月7日]]
（[[弘化]]2年[[8月6日 (旧暦)|8月6日]]） | 出生地 = {{JPN}} [[出雲国]]
（現・[[島根県]]） | 没年月日 = {{death date|1918|1|3|1845|9|7}} | 前職 = [[神道大社教]]管長 | 所属政党 = （[[研究会 (貴族院)|研究会]]→）

[[尾崎洵盛]]（娘婿） | 国旗 = JPN | 職名 = 第14代 [[法務大臣|司法大臣]] | 内閣 = [[第1次西園寺内閣]] | 就任日 = [[1908年]][[3月25日]] | 退任日 = 1908年[[7月14日]] | 国旗2 = 東京都 | 職名2 = 第17代 [[東京都知事一覧|東京府知事]] | 就任日2 = [[1898年]][[11月12日]] | 退任日2 = 1908年3月25日 | 国旗3 = 静岡県 | 職名3 = 第4代 [[静岡県知事]] | 就任日3 = [[1897年]][[4月7日]] | 退任日3 = 1898年[[7月16日]] | 国旗4 = 埼玉県 | 職名4 = 第4代 [[埼玉県知事]] | 就任日4 = [[1894年]][[1月20日]] | 退任日4 = 1897年4月7日 | 国旗5 = JPN | 職名5 = [[貴族院 (日本)|貴族院議員]] | 選挙区5 = [[貴族院男爵議員]] | 当選回数5 = 4回 | 就任日5 = [[1890年]][[7月10日]] – 1897年[[7月9日]]
1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 出雲大社教] * {{Commons category-inline}} {{Clear}} {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} Chairman of the {{ill|Legal commission|ja|法律取調委員会}} | years = 1908 | before = {{ill|Matsuda Masahisa|ja|松田正久}} | after = {{ill|Okabe Nakamoto|ja|岡部長職}} }} {{S-bus}} {{Succession box | title = President of [[Tokyo Toden]] | years = 1909–1911 | before = {{ill|Gengaku Mutaguchi|ja|牟田口元学}} | after = Abolished }} {{S-other}} {{Succession box | title = Head of [[Izumo-taishakyo]] | years = [[1882]]–[[1888]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Sonai (千家尊愛) }} {{Succession box | title = Head Priest of [[Izumo Taisha]] | years = [[1872]]–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{Succession box | title = 80th {{ill|Izumo no kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} | years = 1872–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{S-reg|jp}} {{Succession box | title = Baron | years = [[1884]]–[[1918]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Takamuni (千家尊統) }} {{End}} {{Izumo-taishakyo}} [[category:kk]][[category:Founders of religions]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese Shintoists]] 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 出雲大社教] * {{Commons category-inline}} {{Clear}} {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} Chairman of the {{ill|Legal commission|ja|法律取調委員会}} | years = 1908 | before = Mutaguchi|ja|牟田口元学}} | after = Abolished }} {{S-other}} {{Succession box | title = Head of [[Izumo-taishakyo]] | years = [[1882]]–[[1888]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Sonai (千家尊愛) }} {{Succession box | title = Head Priest of [[Izumo Taisha]] | years = [[1872]]–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{Succession box | title = 80th {{ill|Izumo no kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} | years = 1872–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{S-reg|jp}} {{Succession box | title = Baron | years = [[1884]]–[[1918]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Takamuni (千家尊統) }} {{End}} {{Izumo-taishakyo}} [[category:kk]][[category:Founders of religions]] [[category:kk]][[category:Japanese Shintoists]] 1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. 1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] {{Short description|Japanese politician and religious leader}} * {{Infobox officeholder | 人名 = 千家 尊福 | 各国語表記 = せんげ たかとみ | 画像 = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | 画像サイズ = 240px | 画像説明 = [[大礼服]]で[[正装]]した千家 | 国略称 = {{JPN}} | 生年月日 = [[1845年]][[9月7日]]
（[[弘化]]2年[[8月6日 (旧暦)|8月6日]]） | 出生地 = {{JPN}} [[出雲国]]
（現・[[島根県]]） | 没年月日 = {{death date|1918|1|3|1845|9|7}} | 前職 = [[神道大社教]]管長 | 所属政党 = （[[研究会 (貴族院)|研究会]]→）
[[木曜会 (貴族院)|木曜会]] | 称号・勲章 = [[正二位]]
[[勲一等旭日大綬章]]
[[男爵]] | 子女 = [[千家元麿]]（長男・[[庶子]]）
千家鯱丸（三男・庶子）
[[千家哲麿]]（六男） | 親族（政治家） = [[伏原宣明]]（義祖父）
[[千家尊澄]]（父）
[[千家尊統]]（婿養子）
[[千家紀彦]]（孫）
[[千家国麿]]（玄孫）
[[尾崎洵盛]]（娘婿） | 国旗 = JPN | 職名 = 第14代 [[法務大臣|司法大臣]] | 内閣 = [[第1次西園寺内閣]] | 就任日 = [[1908年]][[3月25日]] | 退任日 = 1908年[[7月14日]] | 国旗2 = 東京都 | 職名2 = 第17代 [[東京都知事一覧|東京府知事]] | 就任日2 = [[1898年]][[11月12日]] | 退任日2 = 1908年3月25日 | 国旗3 = 静岡県 | 職名3 = 第4代 [[静岡県知事]] | 就任日3 = [[1897年]][[4月7日]] | 退任日3 = 1898年[[7月16日]] | 国旗4 = 埼玉県 | 職名4 = 第4代 [[埼玉県知事]] | 就任日4 = [[1894年]][[1月20日]] | 退任日4 = 1897年4月7日 | 国旗5 = JPN | 職名5 = [[貴族院 (日本)|貴族院議員]] | 選挙区5 = [[貴族院男爵議員]] | 当選回数5 = 4回 | 就任日5 = [[1890年]][[7月10日]] – 1897年[[7月9日]]
1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] ==References== {{reflist}} == External links == * [http://www.law.kyushu-u.ac.jp/~shichinohe/minpo/(37)_senge_takatomi.htm 千家尊福 経歴] * [http://www.izumooyashiro.or.jp/hitobito/taisyakyo/index.html 出雲大社教] * {{Commons Category-inline}} {{Clear}} {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} Chairman of the {{ill|Legal commission|ja|法律取調委員会}} | years = 1908 | before = Mutaguchi|ja|牟田口元学}} | after = Abolished }} {{S-other}} {{Succession box | title = Head of [[Izumo-taishakyo]] | years = [[1882]]–[[1888]] | before = Position established | after = Senge Sonai (千家尊愛) }} {{Succession box | title = Head Priest of [[Izumo Taisha]] | years = [[1872]]–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{Succession box | title = 80th {{ill|Izumo no kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|出雲国造}} | years = 1872–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{S-reg|jp}} {{Succession box | title = Baron | years = [[1884]]–[[1918]] | before = 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] {{Short description|Japanese politician and religious leader}} * {{Infobox officeholder | 人名 = 千家 尊福 | 各国語表記 = せんげ たかとみ | 画像 = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | 画像サイズ = 240px | 画像説明 = [[大礼服]]で[[正装]]した千家 | 国略称 = {{JPN}} | 生年月日 = [[1845年]][[9月7日]]
（[[弘化]]2年[[8月6日 (旧暦)|8月6日]]） | 出生地 = {{JPN}} [[出雲国]]
（現・[[島根県]]） | 没年月日 = {{death date|1918|1|3|1845|9|7}} | 前職 = [[神道大社教]]管長 | 所属政党 = （[[研究会 (貴族院)|研究会]]→）

1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] {{Short description|Japanese politician and religious leader}} * {{Infobox officeholder | 人名 = 千家 尊福 | 各国語表記 = せんげ たかとみ | 画像 = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | 画像サイズ = 240px | 画像説明 = [[大礼服]]で[[正装]]した千家 | 国略称 = {{JPN}} | 生年月日 = [[1845年]][[9月7日]]

（現・[[島根県]]） | 没年月日 = {{death date|1918|1|3|1845|9|7}} | 前職 = [[神道大社教]]管長 | 所属政党 = （[[研究会 (貴族院)|研究会]]→）

1897年7月10日 – [[1904年]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911年]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] {{Short description|Japanese politician and religious leader}} * {{Infobox officeholder | 人名 = 千家 尊福 | 各国語表記 = せんげ たかとみ | 画像 = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | 画像サイズ = 240px | 画像説明 = [[大礼服]]で[[正装]]した千家 | 国略称 = {{JPN}} | 生年月日 = [[1845]][[9月7日]]
（[[弘化]]2年[[8月6日 (旧暦)|8月6日]]） | 出生地 = {{JPN}} [[出雲国]]
（現・[[島根県]]） | 没年月日 = {{death date|1918|1|3|1845|9|7}} | 前職 = [[神道大社教]]管長 | 所属政党 = （[[研究会 (貴族院)|研究会]]→）

1897年7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] 1897年7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904年7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911年7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918年1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|出雲国造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]]
