[[尾崎洵盛]]（娘婿） | 国旗 = JPN | 職名 = 第14代 [[法務大臣|司法大臣]] | 内閣 = [[第1次西園寺内閣]] | 就任日 = [[1908]][[3月25日]] | 退任日 = 1908th year [[7月14日]] | 国旗2 = 東京都 | 職名2 = 第17代 [[東京都知事一覧|東京府知事]] | 就任日2 = [[1898]][[11月12日]] | 退任日2 = 1908th year 3月25日 | 国旗3 = 静岡県 | 職名3 = 第4代 [[静岡県知事]] | 就任日3 = [[1897]][[4月7日]] | 退任日3 = 1898th year [[7月16日]] | 国旗4 = 埼玉県 | 職名4 = 第4代 [[埼玉県知事]] | 就任日4 = [[1894]][[1月20日]] | 退任日4 = 1897th year 4月7日 | 国旗5 = JPN | 職名5 = [[貴族院 (日本)|貴族院議員]] | 選挙区5 = [[貴族院男爵議員]] | 当選回数5 = 4回 | 就任日5 = [[1890]][[7月10日]] – 1897th year [[7月9日]]
1897th year 7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904th year 7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, [[:en:Governor of Tokyo|Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 – 1908, and in the [[:en:House of Peers (Japan)|House of Peers]]. He was an organizer of the 1907 [[:en:Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition]].{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Mokuyōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British [[:en:House of Lords|House of Lords]]. He was also the Governor of [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]] from 1897 to 1898, the [[:en:Governor of Tokyo|Governor of Tokyo]] from 1898 to 1908 and the [[:en:Minister of Justice (Japan)|Minister of Justice]] in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] ==References== {{reflist}} == External links == * [[尾崎洵盛]]（娘婿） | 国旗 = JPN | 職名 = 第14代 [[法務大臣|司法大臣]] | 内閣 = [[第1次西園寺内閣]] | 就任日 = [[1908]][[3月25日]] | 退任日 = 1908th year [[7月14日]] | 国旗2 = 東京都 | 職名2 = 第17代 [[東京都知事一覧|東京府知事]] | 就任日2 = [[1898]][[11月12日]] | 退任日2 = 1908th year 3月25日 | 国旗3 = 静岡県 | 職名3 = 第4代 [[静岡県知事]] | 就任日3 = [[1897]][[4月7日]] | 退任日3 = 1898th year [[7月16日]] | 国旗4 = 埼玉県 | 職名4 = 第4代 [[埼玉県知事]] | 就任日4 = [[1894]][[1月20日]] | 退任日4 = 1897th year 4月7日 | 国旗5 = JPN | 職名5 = [[貴族院 (日本)|貴族院議員]] | 選挙区5 = [[貴族院男爵議員]] | 当選回数5 = 4回 | 就任日5 = [[1890]][[7月10日]] – 1897th year [[7月9日]]
1897th year 7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904th year 7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Mokuyōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice}} in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] ==References== {{reflist}} == External links == * [http://www.law.kyushu-u.ac.jp/~shichinohe/minpo/(37)_senge_takatomi.htm 千家尊福 経歴] * [http://www.izumooyashiro.or.jp/hitobito/taisyakyo/index.html 出雲大社教] * {{Commons category-inline}} {{Clear}} title based on wikidata|WD=Q5385930}} | after = {{ill|Okabe Nakamoto|ja|岡部長職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okabe Nakamoto|1=Okabe Nagamoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7400591}} }} = Head of [[Izumo-taishakyo]] | years = {{ill|1882|en|1882|WD=Q7812|lt=1882}}–{{ill|1888|en|1888|WD=Q7829|lt=1888}} | before = Position established | after = Senge Sonai (千家尊愛) }} {{Succession box | title = Head Priest of [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] | years = {{ill|1872|en|1872|WD=Q7753|lt=1872}}–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{Succession box | title = 80th {{ill|Izumo clan|lt=Izumo clan|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|Izumo Province 造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo no kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | years = 1872–1882 | before = Takazumi Senge (千家尊澄) | after = Senge Takaki (千家尊紀) }} {{S-reg|jp}} {{Succession box | title = Baron | years = {{ill|1884|en|1884|WD=Q7819|lt=1884}}–{{ill|1918|en|1918|WD=Q2094|lt=1918}} | Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of （現・[[島根県]]） | 没年月日 = {{death date|1918|1|3|1845|9|7}} | 前職 = [[神道大社教]]管長 | 所属政党 = （[[研究会 (貴族院)|研究会]]→）

[[千家尊澄]]（父）

[[尾崎洵盛]]（娘婿） | 国旗 = JPN | 職名 = 第14代 [[法務大臣|司法大臣]] | 内閣 = [[第1次西園寺内閣]] | 就任日 = [[1908]][[3月25日]] | 退任日 = 1908th year [[7月14日]] | 国旗2 = 東京都 | 職名2 = 第17代 [[東京都知事一覧|東京府知事]] | 就任日2 = [[1898]][[11月12日]] | 退任日2 = 1908th year 3月25日 | 国旗3 = 静岡県 | 職名3 = 第4代 [[静岡県知事]] | 就任日3 = [[1897]][[4月7日]] | 退任日3 = 1898th year [[7月16日]] | 国旗4 = 埼玉県 | 職名4 = 第4代 [[埼玉県知事]] | 就任日4 = [[1894]][[1月20日]] | 退任日4 = 1897th year 4月7日 | 国旗5 = JPN | 職名5 = [[貴族院 (日本)|貴族院議員]] | 選挙区5 = [[貴族院男爵議員]] | 当選回数5 = 4回 | 就任日5 = [[1890]][[7月10日]] – 1897th year [[7月9日]]
1897th year 7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904th year 7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Mokuyōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice}} in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] ==References== {{reflist}} == External links == * Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Mokuyōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice}} in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State 1897th year 7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904th year 7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Mokuyōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice}} in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] ==References== {{reflist}} == External links == * （[[弘化]]2nd year [[8月6日 (旧暦)|8月6日]]） | 出生地 = {{JPN}} [[Izumo Province ]]
（現・[[島根県]]） | 没年月日 = {{death date|1918|1|3|1845|9|7}} | 前職 = [[神道大社教]]管長 | 所属政党 = （[[研究会 (貴族院)|研究会]]→）

[[千家哲麿]]（六男） | 親族（政治家） = [[伏原宣明]]（義祖父）
[[千家尊澄]]（父）
[[千家尊統]]（婿養子）
[[千家紀彦]]（孫）
[[千家国麿]]（玄孫）
[[尾崎洵盛]]（娘婿） | 国旗 = JPN | 職名 = 第14代 [[法務大臣|司法大臣]] | 内閣 = [[第1次西園寺内閣]] | 就任日 = [[1908]][[3月25日]] | 退任日 = 1908th year [[7月14日]] | 国旗2 = 東京都 | 職名2 = 第17代 [[東京都知事一覧|東京府知事]] | 就任日2 = [[1898]][[11月12日]] | 退任日2 = 1908th year 3月25日 | 国旗3 = 静岡県 | 職名3 = 第4代 [[静岡県知事]] | 就任日3 = [[1897]][[4月7日]] | 退任日3 = 1898th year [[7月16日]] | 国旗4 = 埼玉県 | 職名4 = 第4代 [[埼玉県知事]] | 就任日4 = [[1894]][[1月20日]] | 退任日4 = 1897th year 4月7日 | 国旗5 = JPN | 職名5 = [[貴族院 (日本)|貴族院議員]] | 選挙区5 = [[貴族院男爵議員]] | 当選回数5 = 4回 | 就任日5 = [[1890]][[7月10日]] – 1897th year [[7月9日]]
1897th year 7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904th year 7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Mokuyōkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice}} in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] ==References== {{reflist}} == External links == * He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords|1=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice|1=Minister of Justice (Japan)}} in 1908. == See 1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers|1=House of Peers (Japan)}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|1=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Q11518492|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords|1=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice|1=Minister of Justice (Japan)}} in 1908. == See He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers|1=House of Peers (Japan)}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|1=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Q11518492|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords|1=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice|1=Minister of Justice (Japan)}} in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] * [[Noriko Senge]] ==References== {{reflist}} == External links == * [http://www.law.kyushu-u.ac.jp/~shichinohe/minpo/(37)_senge_takatomi.htm 千家尊福 経歴] * {{ill|Matsuda Masahisa|ja|松田正久|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuda Masahisa|1=Matsuda Masahisa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5385930}} | after = {{ill|Okabe 1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers|1=House of Peers (Japan)}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|1=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Q11518492|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. [[尾崎洵盛]]（娘婿） | 国旗 = JPN | 職名 = 第14代 [[法務大臣|司法大臣]] | 内閣 = [[第1次西園寺内閣]] | 就任日 = [[1908]][[3月25日]] | 退任日 = 1908th year [[7月14日]] | 国旗2 = 東京都 | 職名2 = 第17代 [[東京都知事一覧|東京府知事]] | 就任日2 = [[1898]][[11月12日]] | 退任日2 = 1908th year 3月25日 | 国旗3 = 静岡県 | 職名3 = 第4代 [[静岡県知事]] | 就任日3 = [[1897]][[4月7日]] | 退任日3 = 1898th year [[7月16日]] | 国旗4 = 埼玉県 | 職名4 = 第4代 [[埼玉県知事]] | 就任日4 = [[1894]][[1月20日]] | 退任日4 = 1897th year 4月7日 | 国旗5 = JPN | 職名5 = [[貴族院 (日本)|貴族院議員]] | 選挙区5 = [[貴族院男爵議員]] | 当選回数5 = 4回 | 就任日5 = [[1890]][[7月10日]] – 1897th year [[7月9日]]
1897th year 7月10日 – [[1904]]7月9日
1904th year 7月10日 – [[1911]]7月9日
1911th year 7月10日 | 退任日5 = 1918th year 1月3日 | image = Takatomi Senge 01.jpg | birth_date = 7 September 1845 | death_date = 3 January 1918 }} Baron '''Takatomi Senge''' was a Japanese priest and politician. He served as governor of two prefectures, {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 – 1908, and in the {{ill|House of Peers (Japan)|en|House of Peers (Japan)|WD=Q715139|lt=House of Peers|1=House of Peers (Japan)}}. He was an organizer of the 1907 {{ill|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|en|Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|WD=Q119285886|lt=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition|1=Tokyo Industrial Exhibition}}.{{cite web | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=j0BBAQAAMAAJ&dq=Tokyo+Industrial+Exhibition+1907&pg=PA287 | title=The Far Eastern Review: Engineering commerce, finance | year=1906 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.edohakuarchives.jp/detail-23749.html | title=Baron Senge Takatomi, President of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition Support Association; Ozaki Yukio, Vice-President; Baron Sone Arasuke, Chief Examiner of the Tokyo Industrial Exhibition | EDO-TOKYO MUSEIUM Digital Archives }} He was a Shinto Priest and the founder of [[Izumo-taishakyo]], and the 80th high priest of [[Izumo-taisha]]{{cite book |author=Miller, R.A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=M4ba4AeaFHAC |title=現代日本文読本: Graded Lessons for Mastering the Written Language |date=1962 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=9780804816472 |page=115 |accessdate=2015-01-01}}{{cite book |author1=Teeuwen, M. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yBFFAz4z3gUC |title=Shinto, a Short History |author2=Breen, J. |author3=Inoue, N. |author4=Mori, M. |date=2003 |publisher=RoutledgeCurzon |isbn=9780415311793 |page=177 |accessdate=2015-01-01}} (an eightieth generation descendant of the first [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]).https://archive.today/20230527183732/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9470 He founded the {{Nihongo3|The Thursday Association|木曜会|{{ill|Mokuyōkai|ja|木曜会 (貴族院)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mokuyōkai|1=Q11518492|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11518492}}}} a Japanese political faction dedicated to representing the interests of barons over higher nobility. == Ancestry == {{Main|Izumo-taisha}} The descendants of {{Nihongo|[[:en:Amenohohi-no-mikoto|Amenohohi-no-mikoto]]|天穂日命}}, the second son of {{Nihongo|[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-ōmikami]]|天照大御神}}, the sun goddess whose first son is the ancestor of the imperial family, have been, in the name of {{Nihongo|Izumo Kokuso|Izumo Province 造}} or governor of Izumo, taking over rituals because when Izumo-taisha was founded Amenohohi-no-mikoto rendered service to Okuninushi-no-kami. The family's conflict around 1340 made them separated into two lineages, {{Nihongo|Senge|千家}} and {{Nihongo|Kitajima|北島}}."Japantimes – Princess Noriko to wed" [http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/05/27/national/princess-noriko-to-wed-son-of-izumo-taisha-chief-priest/#.VC_BNCjLZ5g], Tokyo, 27 May 2014. Retrieved on 4 October 2014 In 1871, Izumo-taisha was designated as an Imperial-associated shrine and the government sent a new administrator so Izumo kokuso families were no more the administrators of Izumo-taisha. So Senge's father was exiled and Takatomi Senge never managed to be high priest of the shrine. Senge reorganized the traditions of Izumo Taisha under the organization [[Izumo-taishakyo]] and was instrumental in the development of [[Sect Shinto]]. It was only in 1947 under the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Allied Occupation of Japan]] that the Senge family managed to become high priests of Izumo Taisha again. {{Nihongo|Takatoshi Senge|千家尊祀}}, the 83rd-generation [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] of Senge lineage, was chosen to be the chief priest of Izumo-taisha in 1947. He died in February 2002 at the age of 89. == Political offices == Senge held many political offices in his life. He was a member of Japan's House of Peers, a group of nobles who were like the British {{ill|House of Lords|en|House of Lords|WD=Q11007|lt=House of Lords|1=House of Lords}}. He was also the Governor of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}} from 1894 to 1897, the Governor of {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}} from 1897 to 1898, the {{ill|Governor of Tokyo|en|Governor of Tokyo|WD=Q11525354|lt=Governor of Tokyo|1=Governor of Tokyo}} from 1898 to 1908 and the {{ill|Minister of Justice (Japan)|en|Minister of Justice (Japan)|WD=Q841904|lt=Minister of Justice|1=Minister of Justice (Japan)}} in 1908. == See also == * [[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} * [[Izumo-taisha]] * [[Izumo-taishakyo]] * [[Sect Shinto]] *
